title: Total Waviness of Numbers in Range I
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ proposals for Range I. The recommended digit DP is the fast-and-lean default, the runtime proposal uses a full-domain prefix table, and the memory proposal keeps the hinted direct scan.

variants.languageReason: The fast-and-lean objective is best served by C++ fixed arrays and branch-light integer transitions. The digit DP touches only a few hundred states for two prefix bounds, avoids heap allocation, and keeps the method independent of interval length.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected: native loops, compact fixed arrays, inlining, and direct compatibility with the LeetCode class signature.
Considered for minimal runtime overhead, but LeetCode's C++ class interface is more natural and C does not improve the fixed-state DP meaningfully.
Considered for native performance and memory safety, but wrapper friction and bounds-check noise are not worthwhile for six-digit fixed arrays.
Considered for compiled loops, but runtime initialization and bounds-check constants are less attractive for this tiny branch-heavy DP.
Considered for JIT speed, but startup and managed runtime overhead do not help a method dominated by a few hundred integer transitions.
Accepted brute force is possible under the small constraint, but interpreter or VM overhead is weaker for the top-1% target.

variants.approachJustification: Compute prefix(n) with digit DP. Track meaningful digit count capped at two, previous digit, and previous comparison sign; a non-zero sign flip adds one wave for every suffix completion.

variants.languageReason: C++ can lazily initialize a static prefix array over the full 0..100000 domain with tight arithmetic loops, then answer each LeetCode call with two array reads and one subtraction.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected: static array initialization is compact, cache-friendly, and directly supports constant-time calls after setup.
Considered for manual static storage, but it does not fit the C++ class signature as cleanly and offers no relevant asymptotic gain.
Considered for native loops, but global lazy initialization is more verbose and not faster in this judge context.
Considered for package-level tables, but runtime startup and bounds checks are less favorable than C++ static arrays.
Considered for static arrays, but managed initialization and runtime overhead are heavier for a 100001-cell table.
Could precompute the same table, but per-element interpreter or VM overhead makes setup slower.

variants.approachJustification: Precompute cumulative waviness for every value up to the maximum constraint once. The query answer is pref[num2] - pref[num1 - 1].
variants.timeComplexity: O(100000 * D) setup, O(1) per query

variants.languageReason: The memory objective only needs scalar integer state. C++ arithmetic digit extraction avoids strings, dynamic arrays, garbage collection, and per-number object allocation.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected: scalar loops and private static helpers give the same memory footprint as a C-style implementation while matching LeetCode ergonomics.
Considered for the absolute bare-metal style, but the LeetCode problem is naturally submitted through C++ here and the scalar memory footprint is already minimal.
Considered for stack-only native code, but it adds verbosity without reducing auxiliary memory below the C++ scalar scan.
Considered for simple integer loops, but runtime overhead makes reported memory larger than the scalar C++ solution.
Considered but rejected for the least-memory axis because the managed runtime dominates the few scalar variables used by the algorithm.
Considered due to small constraints, but boxed or dynamic numeric/runtime representations are much heavier than scalar C++ integers.

variants.approachJustification: Scan every number from max(num1, 100) to num2 and extract digits arithmetically, keeping only left/middle/right rolling digits.

reasoningSummary: A middle digit is a wave when adjacent comparison signs flip between +1 and -1. The recommended solution counts waves in bulk with prefix digit DP; the speed extreme precomputes the full constrained domain; the memory extreme follows the hinted direct scan with scalar arithmetic digit extraction.
timeComplexity: O(D) recommended / O(1) per query after setup runtime / O(R * D) memory
spaceComplexity: O(1) recommended / O(100000) runtime / O(1) memory
